subject: Computer Networking
title: The Interface Message Processor
description: Learn The Interface Message Processor in one minute.

hook.text: ARPANET's computers were all different. A special machine sat between each one and the network — the IMP.

buildup.text: IMPs were refrigerator-sized routers. They handled packets so the host computers didn't have to.

discovery.text: BBN Technologies built the first IMPs in 1969. Each weighed about 900 pounds and cost hundreds of thousands.

twist.text: The IMP concept — separating networking from computing — is exactly how modern routers still work today.

climax.text: As ARPANET grew, IMPs were deployed at universities and labs. They became the physical backbone of the network.

punchline.text: IMPs were the first routers. Big, heavy, and revolutionary.

quiz.question: What was the role of an IMP in ARPANET?
options: It handled packet routing so host computers didn't have to
It stored all email messages for the network
It generated electricity for the network nodes
